questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes a blockchain?

options.text: A centralised database managed by a single company with strong encryption
A distributed, append-only ledger maintained by a peer-to-peer network and secured by cryptography
A cloud storage system where files are encrypted and backed up automatically
A type of database that allows very fast read and write operations across multiple servers

questions.explanation: Option B captures all three defining properties: distributed (peer-to-peer network), append-only (immutable), and cryptographically secured. Option A is wrong - centralised databases with encryption are not blockchains; the key feature is the distributed architecture. Option C describes a cloud backup service. Option D describes a distributed database (like Cassandra or DynamoDB), which still allows UPDATE and DELETE operations - the exact opposite of append-only.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Explanation

Option B captures all three defining properties: distributed (peer-to-peer network), append-only (immutable), and cryptographically secured. Option A is wrong - centralised databases with encryption are not blockchains; the key feature is the distributed architecture. Option C describes a cloud backup service. Option D describes a distributed database (like Cassandra or DynamoDB), which still allows UPDATE and DELETE operations - the exact opposite of append-only.

Examiner note: Watch for options that include the word "encrypted" - encryption alone does not make a blockchain.

questions.text: The "double-spend problem" that blockchain solves refers to:

options.text: Spending twice as much money as you have in your bank account
A hacker intercepting your transaction and stealing your private key
The risk of spending the same unit of digital currency in two separate transactions simultaneously
Paying double the gas fees due to network congestion

questions.explanation: Digital data is trivially copyable. Before blockchain, if Ravi had a ₹100 digital file, he could send the same file to both Priya and Rahul - effectively spending the same ₹100 twice. Banks prevented this by maintaining a central ledger that deducted on first spend. Blockchain prevents it through public consensus: once a transaction spending coin X is included in a block, every node knows coin X is spent, and any second transaction attempting to spend it is immediately rejected by the network.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

Digital data is trivially copyable. Before blockchain, if Ravi had a ₹100 digital file, he could send the same file to both Priya and Rahul - effectively spending the same ₹100 twice. Banks prevented this by maintaining a central ledger that deducted on first spend. Blockchain prevents it through public consensus: once a transaction spending coin X is included in a block, every node knows coin X is spent, and any second transaction attempting to spend it is immediately rejected by the network.

Common trap: Option A describes overdraft - a banking problem unrelated to blockchain's design purpose.

questions.text: Which of the following SQL operations is IMPOSSIBLE on a public blockchain?

options.text: INSERT - adding a new transaction to the chain
SELECT - reading any transaction ever recorded
DELETE - removing a transaction from Block #1
SELECT - querying the current balance of any wallet address

questions.explanation: Blockchain is append-only. This means INSERT (append a new block) is possible, and SELECT (read any historical record) is possible on a public chain. But DELETE and UPDATE are architecturally impossible - removing or changing Block #1 would change its cryptographic hash, which would invalidate Block #2's PrevHash field, cascading through every subsequent block. The honest network would immediately detect and reject the tampered chain. There is no administrator with a DELETE privilege - the architecture itself prevents it.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

Blockchain is append-only. This means INSERT (append a new block) is possible, and SELECT (read any historical record) is possible on a public chain. But DELETE and UPDATE are architecturally impossible - removing or changing Block #1 would change its cryptographic hash, which would invalidate Block #2's PrevHash field, cascading through every subsequent block. The honest network would immediately detect and reject the tampered chain. There is no administrator with a DELETE privilege - the architecture itself prevents it.

Note: Options A, B, and D describe valid operations on blockchain: you can write (append) new transactions and read any historical data.

questions.text: "A blockchain is essentially a more secure version of a traditional database - they work in the same fundamental way, but blockchain adds stronger encryption and access controls."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and mentions at least one architectural difference (append-only, no central admin, hash chain).+1 mark: Provides a coherent justification that explains why this difference is structural rather than just a security upgrade - covering at least two of: (a) append-only vs UPDATE/DELETE, (b) distributed vs centralised, (c) cryptographic hash chain enforcement vs access controls.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and mentions at least one architectural difference (append-only, no central admin, hash chain).

+1 mark: Provides a coherent justification that explains why this difference is structural rather than just a security upgrade - covering at least two of: (a) append-only vs UPDATE/DELETE, (b) distributed vs centralised, (c) cryptographic hash chain enforcement vs access controls.

Examiner note: Simply writing "False - because blockchain uses cryptography" earns 0 marks. The justification must address the structural/architectural difference.
